fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from devs using our CLI toolkit that they keep having to write the same boilerplate argument scanning code over and over again. Every project reinvents the wheel — someone parses --long flags, someone else handles -s short forms, and nobody handles the edge cases the same way (like what happens when you pass a number that's too big, or forget to give a value after a flag). We need a proper library that lets you just declare what options your program accepts and get back a clean result object you can query.

Remember how we handled the 'compatibility logic' for grouped short flags in that config-loader module last quarter? Something like that approach would work here. Users should be able to bundle short flags together, mix positional arguments with named ones, and get sensible errors back when something goes wrong — not a crash or a silent wrong value.

We also want support for things like 'if the flag is missing, use this fallback' and 'if the flag is present but has no value attached, use this other fallback.' Numeric stuff needs to handle hex, negatives, overflow — the usual. Oh and there should be some kind of help/synopsis output. The JSON adapter contract we discussed internally should stay completely separate from the core engine. Please refer to how we scoped the iteration and optional-value patterns in the last sprint.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: ParseResult parse(int argc, const char* argv[])

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Short flags may be bundled into a single token (e.g., `-abc`). The last short option in the bundle, if it is value-bearing, absorbs the remaining characters of the token as its value (e.g., `-bcfoo=something` sets `b` as a boolean flag and `c` to the value `foo=something`). Non-final short flags in a bundle must be boolean/non-value-bearing.

A default value applies only when the option is entirely absent from the command line. Its rendered value is the default text, but its count is 0 (it was never seen). This is distinct from an implicit value, which applies when the option is present but given an empty equals-assignment (`--name=`).

trigger_keywords: flag present but no value attached
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An implicit value is used when a value-bearing option is given an empty equals-assignment (e.g., `--name=`). This produces a present value of the empty string with a count of 1. When `no_implicit` is set to true on an option, supplying the bare flag with no argument fails with `missing_argument` naming the option.

A positional mapping names an ordered list of declared options. Bare tokens are assigned to those names in sequence. The final mapped name, if list-valued, collects all remaining bare tokens. Tokens after a bare `--` separator feed the list if a positional mapping exists, otherwise become unmatched. List-valued positionals keep comma characters verbatim — they are NOT split on commas. If a positional name does not correspond to any declared option, parsing fails with `no_such_option` naming that token.

Integer-typed options accept decimal (including negative and leading-zero forms like `05`) and hexadecimal (`0x`-prefixed, any case). Conversions are bounds-checked against the declared integer width (e.g., `vec_int8` rejects 128). A value beyond the representable range, a negative value for an unsigned type, or a token with non-numeric characters is rejected with `incorrect_argument_type` naming the offending token.

The defined error categories are: `no_such_option` (unknown option name), `invalid_option_syntax` (malformed token, e.g., `--a` single-char in long form), `invalid_option_format` (bad declaration, e.g., empty opts string), `missing_argument` (value-bearing option with no_implicit given bare), `incorrect_argument_type` (value fails type conversion or range check), `option_has_no_value` (querying typed value on absent option with no default). The error output is two lines: `error=<category>` and `param=<offending token>` (param line omitted when no token is relevant).

By default, an unknown option token aborts parsing with `no_such_option`. When `allow_unrecognised` is set to true, unknown tokens (including the unmatched tail of a grouped short run and malformed short tokens) are collected into the unmatched list instead of aborting. A single-character token written in long form (e.g., `--a`) is always `invalid_option_syntax` regardless of allow_unrecognised.

Iterating the result visits each present option in command-line order, followed by any options that contributed a default value (but were not seen on the command line). Options with neither a command-line occurrence nor a default are excluded. Each entry is rendered as `iter=<canonical_key>:<value>`, terminated by `iter_end`.

Options may be declared with an associated group name. The number of distinct groups is reported via the `groups` query. A configuration with no declared options has zero groups. An empty-string group name and a named group name count as distinct groups when both are present.

The usage synopsis line is built from the program name plus a configurable positional-help fragment. When a custom positional-help fragment is provided and the default option-list placeholder is suppressed (custom_help set to empty string), the synopsis line is exactly `<program_name> <positional_help_fragment>`. The query key is `usage_line`.

An option whose value type is itself an optional (e.g., `opt_string`, `opt_bool`) records the parsed value when supplied and exposes whether the optional holds a value. Querying such an option returns the contained value when present. A boolean optional with a default of false records true when explicitly set to true on the command line.

trigger_keywords: which name is used in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each recognised argument's canonical key in ordered argument output is the option's first long name as declared in the `opts` specification. If the option has no long name (only a short single-character name), the short name is used as the canonical key. All aliases of the same option share one count and one value.

trigger_keywords: appending to list on multiple appearances
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Repeating a list-valued option on the command line accumulates all values in order. For example, `-j 9 --job 7 --job=10 -j5` produces `[9,7,10,5]` and a count of 4. The value may be attached directly to the token (e.g., `--job=10` or `-j5`) or supplied as the next token.

The core parsing engine must remain completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON input/output contract is a black-box testing interface for the execution adapter only. The adapter translates JSON commands into idiomatic calls to the engine and renders results as output lines. The engine itself exposes a typed API: register options, parse, query — with no awareness of JSON or console I/O.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic for grouped short flags in that config-loader module last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The short-option bundling logic in the argument scanner: `-bcfoo=something` sets `b` as boolean and `c` to value `foo=something`; implemented in the tokenisation/scanning layer of the parsing engine (feature1_basic_option_parsing.json, second test case)

context_pointers.vague_reference: iteration and optional-value patterns in the last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `iterate` query (feature8_result_inspection.json, third test case) and `opt_string`/`opt_bool` optional-typed value support (feature9_optional_typed_values.json), including the `iter=key:value` / `iter_end` line format and `optional[name]=<none>` defensive read pattern

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — you'll need to check the existing codebase and test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` to confirm the exact expected behavior. Look at the relevant feature JSON file for the input/output contract.

feature10_usage_synopsis.json.description: Usage synopsis rendering. The generated help text contains a usage synopsis line built from the program name plus a configurable positional-help fragment. When the default option-list placeholder is suppressed and a custom positional-help fragment is provided, the synopsis line reflects exactly the program name followed by that fragment.

feature1_basic_option_parsing.json.description: Basic option recognition: a single declaration list may expose an option under several long names and an optional short name. Parsing the argument vector records how many times each alias was seen (counts are shared across aliases of the same option), preserves the ordered list of recognized arguments (each with a canonical key and rendered value), and exposes the program name. Short options may be grouped into a single token, and the last grouped short may absorb the remaining characters as its value.

cases.expected_output:
count[long]=1
contains[long]=true
count[value]=1
value[value]=value
value[a]=b
count[p]=2
count[space]=2
count[quick]=2
count[f]=2
count[z]=4
count[period.delimited]=1
count[nothing]=0
contains[nothing]=false
program=tester

count[a]=1
value[a]=value
count[b]=1
count[c]=1
value[c]=foo=something
arguments=3
arg[0]=a:value
arg[1]=b:true
arg[2]=c:foo=something

feature2_positional_arguments.json.description: Positional argument mapping: a named set of options can be designated as positional, so bare tokens on the command line are assigned to them in order. A trailing positional declared as a list collects all remaining tokens. Tokens after a bare double-dash separator, or tokens with no positional mapping, are reported as unmatched. List-valued positionals retain comma characters verbatim (no splitting). When the only mapped positional name does not correspond to a declared option, parsing reports a no_such_option error.

feature3_implicit_and_default_values.jso.description: Implicit and default values. An option may declare an implicit value used when the flag is given an empty equals-assignment, and a default value used when the flag is absent entirely. When a value-bearing option is given as `--name=` (empty assignment) it records a present value of the empty string. A boolean option configured with no implicit value requires an explicit argument: supplying the bare flag reports a missing_argument error, while explicit truthy/falsy assignments parse correctly. Default values apply with a count of zero when the option is absent.

feature4_numeric_value_parsing.json.description: Numeric value parsing. Integer-typed options accept decimal (including negative and leading-zero forms) and hexadecimal (0x-prefixed) notations, normalizing them to their numeric value. Signed and unsigned widths are bounds-checked: values at the boundary parse, while values outside the representable range, negative values for unsigned types, and non-numeric tokens are rejected as incorrect_argument_type. Floating-point options accept decimal and scientific notation including negatives.

feature5_boolean_and_list_values.json.description: Boolean and list value parsing. Boolean options accept several truthy and falsy spellings: bare presence, `=true`/`=false`, `=1`/`=0`, and absence with an explicit default. List-valued options split a single comma-delimited argument into multiple typed elements. A boolean option absent from the command line and lacking an explicit default reports as false with a count of zero.

cases.expected_output:
count[bool]=1
value[bool]=false
value[debug]=true
value[timing]=true
value[verbose]=true
value[dry-run]=false
value[noExplicitDefault]=false
value[defaultTrue]=true
value[defaultFalse]=false
count[others]=1

feature6_unrecognised_and_invalid_option.description: Handling of unrecognized and malformed options. By default an unknown option token aborts parsing with a no_such_option error, and a malformed short-option token aborts with an invalid_option_syntax error; a single-character token written in long form (`--a`) is likewise an invalid_option_syntax error. When unrecognized options are explicitly allowed, unknown tokens (including the unmatched tail of a grouped short run and malformed short tokens) are collected into the unmatched list instead of aborting.

feature7_option_declaration_and_groups.j.description: Option declaration variants and grouping. Options may be declared with an associated group name, a per-option default value, and an argument-help label; the number of distinct groups is reported via groups(). A single declaration may expose several long names plus a short name, all sharing one underlying value. Declaring an option with an empty specification is rejected as invalid_option_format. A configuration with no declared options has zero groups.

cases.expected_output:
groups=2
count[address]=1
count[port]=1
count[test]=1
count[help]=0
value[address]=10.0.0.1
value[port]=8000
value[test]=true

feature8_result_inspection.json.description: Reading back parse results. A value not declared on the command line and lacking a default has no value: querying its typed value reports an option_has_no_value error. A value-bearing option may be queried defensively as optional, yielding the value when present and a none marker when absent. Iterating the result visits each present option in command-line order followed by any options that contributed a default value, exposing each as a key/value pair. Repeating a list-valued option (with and without the value attached to the token) accumulates all values in order.

cases.expected_output:
iter=long:true
iter=short:true
iter=a:true
iter=value:value
iter=default:42
iter_end

feature9_optional_typed_values.json.description: Optional-typed values. An option whose value type is itself an optional records the parsed value when supplied, and exposes whether the optional holds a value. A string optional carries the supplied text; a boolean optional with a default of false records true when explicitly set. Querying such an option returns the contained value when present.
